questTitle: O-Level Physics: Effects of Heating - Expansion and Contraction Worksheet 2
subject: Physics
description: Master the effects of heating! This worksheet covers thermal expansion and contraction in solids, liquids, and gases, particle theory explanations, applications, problems caused by expansion, and practical examples. Show clear understanding of concepts!

questions.question: What generally happens to the size of most substances when they are heated?
options: They only change shape, not size
questions.answer: They expand (get larger)

questions.question: What generally happens to the size of most substances when they are cooled?
questions.answer: They contract (get smaller)

questions.question: Using particle theory, explain why substances expand when heated.
options: More particles are added when heated
Particles gain kinetic energy, vibrate more vigorously, and move further apart on average, increasing the volume
questions.answer: Particles gain kinetic energy, vibrate more vigorously, and move further apart on average, increasing the volume

questions.question: Using particle theory, explain why substances contract when cooled.
options: Particles lose kinetic energy, vibrate less vigorously, and move closer together on average, decreasing the volume
questions.answer: Particles lose kinetic energy, vibrate less vigorously, and move closer together on average, decreasing the volume

questions.question: When a metal ball is heated, what happens to its volume?
questions.answer: Volume increases

questions.question: A metal lid is stuck on a glass jar. To open it, you could run hot water over the lid. Why does this work?
options: The glass expands more than the metal, loosening the lid
The metal lid expands more than the glass, loosening the grip on the jar
The hot water dissolves the lid
The hot water melts the lid
questions.answer: The metal lid expands more than the glass, loosening the grip on the jar

questions.question: Do all solids expand by the same amount when heated by the same temperature increase?
options: No, different materials have different rates of expansion (different coefficients of expansion)
Solids do not expand when heated
questions.answer: No, different materials have different rates of expansion (different coefficients of expansion)

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 1: Expansion of Solids ⭐ Experimental Question
questions.question: A ball-and-ring experiment involves a metal ball that just passes through a metal ring at room temperature. After heating the ball, what is observed and why?
options: The ball passes through more easily because it shrinks
The ball no longer passes through because it has expanded
questions.answer: The ball no longer passes through because it has expanded

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 1: Expansion of Solids ⭐ Experimental Question
questions.question: In the ball-and-ring experiment, if the ring is heated instead of the ball, what happens?
options: The ball will no longer fit through
The hole in the ring gets smaller
The hole in the ring gets larger (the ring expands including the hole), so the ball passes through more easily
questions.answer: The hole in the ring gets larger (the ring expands including the hole), so the ball passes through more easily

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 1: Expansion of Solids - Bimetallic Strip
questions.question: A bimetallic strip is made of two different metals bonded together. When heated, what happens to the strip?
options: Both metals expand equally and the strip stays straight
The strip bends towards the metal that expands more
The strip bends towards the metal that expands less (this metal is on the inside of the curve)
questions.answer: The strip bends towards the metal that expands less (this metal is on the inside of the curve)

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 1: Expansion of Solids - Bimetallic Strip
questions.question: A bimetallic strip is made of brass (expands more) and iron (expands less). When heated, which way does it bend?
options: Towards the iron side (iron is on the inside of the curve as it expands less)
questions.answer: Towards the iron side (iron is on the inside of the curve as it expands less)

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 1: Expansion of Solids - Bimetallic Strip
questions.question: When a bimetallic strip is cooled below room temperature, what happens?
options: It bends the same way as when heated
It bends in the opposite direction (the metal that contracts more is now on the inside)
questions.answer: It bends in the opposite direction (the metal that contracts more is now on the inside)

questions.question: Compare the expansion of liquids to solids for the same temperature increase.
questions.answer: Liquids expand more than solids

questions.question: Why do liquids expand more than solids?
options: Liquid particles are further apart and less tightly held, allowing more expansion when kinetic energy increases
questions.answer: Liquid particles are further apart and less tightly held, allowing more expansion when kinetic energy increases

questions.question: A liquid thermometer works based on which principle?
options: Conduction of heat through the glass
Thermal expansion of the liquid - as temperature increases, the liquid expands and rises up the tube
questions.answer: Thermal expansion of the liquid - as temperature increases, the liquid expands and rises up the tube

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 2: Expansion of Liquids - Anomalous Expansion of Water
questions.question: Water behaves unusually between 0°C and 4°C. What happens to water as it is cooled from 4°C to 0°C?
questions.answer: Water expands (its density decreases)

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 2: Expansion of Liquids - Anomalous Expansion of Water
questions.question: At what temperature does water have its maximum density?

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 2: Expansion of Liquids - Anomalous Expansion of Water
questions.question: Why is the anomalous expansion of water important for aquatic life in cold climates?
options: Water at 4°C sinks to the bottom, ice forms on top, insulating the water below and allowing aquatic life to survive
questions.answer: Water at 4°C sinks to the bottom, ice forms on top, insulating the water below and allowing aquatic life to survive

questions.question: Compare the expansion of gases to liquids and solids for the same temperature increase.
options: Gases expand less than liquids and solids
Gases expand more than liquids, which expand more than solids
questions.answer: Gases expand more than liquids, which expand more than solids

questions.question: Why do gases expand much more than liquids and solids?
options: Gas particles have much weaker forces between them and are much further apart, allowing much greater expansion when kinetic energy increases
questions.answer: Gas particles have much weaker forces between them and are much further apart, allowing much greater expansion when kinetic energy increases

questions.question: A balloon filled with air is placed in a refrigerator. What happens to the balloon and why?
options: The balloon expands because cold air is denser
The balloon shrinks because the air inside contracts as particles lose kinetic energy and move slower
questions.answer: The balloon shrinks because the air inside contracts as particles lose kinetic energy and move slower

questions.question: A sealed plastic bottle is left in a car on a hot sunny day. After some time, the bottle appears inflated. Why?
options: More air has entered the bottle
The air inside has expanded due to the increase in temperature
questions.answer: The air inside has expanded due to the increase in temperature

questions.question: Rivets are used to join metal plates together. The rivet is heated, inserted through holes in the plates, and the end is hammered flat. As the rivet cools, what happens?
options: The rivet expands and falls out
The rivet contracts and pulls the plates tightly together
questions.answer: The rivet contracts and pulls the plates tightly together

questions.question: Metal tyres are fitted onto train wheels when hot. Why?
options: Hot metal is softer and easier to shape
The tyre expands when heated, fits over the wheel, and contracts when cooled to grip the wheel tightly
questions.answer: The tyre expands when heated, fits over the wheel, and contracts when cooled to grip the wheel tightly

questions.question: Fire alarms often use a bimetallic strip. How does this work?
options: The strip melts in a fire
When heated by fire, the bimetallic strip bends, completing an electrical circuit and triggering the alarm
questions.answer: When heated by fire, the bimetallic strip bends, completing an electrical circuit and triggering the alarm

questions.question: Thermostats in appliances (like irons, ovens, water heaters) use bimetallic strips. How do they maintain a constant temperature?
options: They measure temperature with a liquid
When temperature rises too high, the strip bends and breaks the circuit, turning off the heater. When it cools, the strip straightens, reconnecting the circuit
questions.answer: When temperature rises too high, the strip bends and breaks the circuit, turning off the heater. When it cools, the strip straightens, reconnecting the circuit

questions.question: A liquid-in-glass thermometer uses mercury or coloured alcohol. Why must the bore (tube) be very narrow?
options: A narrow bore means a small volume change produces a large change in liquid level, making the thermometer more sensitive
Wide bores are harder to manufacture
To prevent the liquid from evaporating
questions.answer: A narrow bore means a small volume change produces a large change in liquid level, making the thermometer more sensitive

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 5: Problems Caused by Expansion and Solutions
questions.question: Railway tracks have small gaps between sections. Why?
options: To allow for expansion in hot weather; without gaps, the tracks would buckle
questions.answer: To allow for expansion in hot weather; without gaps, the tracks would buckle

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 5: Problems Caused by Expansion and Solutions
questions.question: What would happen if railway tracks were laid without expansion gaps on a hot day?
options: The tracks would contract and break
The tracks would expand and buckle (bend out of shape), potentially causing derailments
questions.answer: The tracks would expand and buckle (bend out of shape), potentially causing derailments

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 5: Problems Caused by Expansion and Solutions
questions.question: Bridges have expansion joints (gaps with interlocking 'teeth'). Why?
options: To allow the bridge to expand and contract with temperature changes without damaging the structure
questions.answer: To allow the bridge to expand and contract with temperature changes without damaging the structure

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 5: Problems Caused by Expansion and Solutions
questions.question: Concrete roads and pavements have expansion joints (gaps filled with flexible material). What happens if these joints are not included?
options: The concrete may crack or buckle as it expands in hot weather with no room to expand
questions.answer: The concrete may crack or buckle as it expands in hot weather with no room to expand

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 5: Problems Caused by Expansion and Solutions
questions.question: Power lines are hung loosely between poles, forming a slight sag. Why?
options: To allow for contraction in cold weather; if too tight, they would snap when they contract
Because tight wires are harder to install
questions.answer: To allow for contraction in cold weather; if too tight, they would snap when they contract

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 5: Problems Caused by Expansion and Solutions
questions.question: When you pour boiling water into a thick glass container, it may crack. Why?
options: The inside of the glass expands faster than the outside, creating stress that causes cracking
The water reacts with the glass
questions.answer: The inside of the glass expands faster than the outside, creating stress that causes cracking

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 5: Problems Caused by Expansion and Solutions
questions.question: Pyrex glass is used for laboratory equipment and ovenware. Why is it less likely to crack with temperature changes?
options: Pyrex has a very low coefficient of expansion, so it expands very little when heated
questions.answer: Pyrex has a very low coefficient of expansion, so it expands very little when heated

questions.question: Arrange solids, liquids, and gases in order of increasing expansion for the same temperature increase.
questions.answer: Solids < Liquids < Gases

questions.question: Why do gases expand much more than solids for the same temperature increase?
options: In gases, particles are much further apart with very weak forces between them, allowing much more movement apart when energy increases
questions.answer: In gases, particles are much further apart with very weak forces between them, allowing much more movement apart when energy increases

questions.question: Two metal rods of the same length are heated by the same amount. Rod A (aluminium) expands more than Rod B (steel). What does this tell us?
options: Aluminium has a higher coefficient of (thermal) expansion than steel
questions.answer: Aluminium has a higher coefficient of (thermal) expansion than steel

questions.question: A glass bottle is completely filled with water and sealed. The bottle is then heated. What is likely to happen and why?
options: The water contracts and creates a vacuum
The water expands more than the glass, pressure builds up, and the bottle may crack or the stopper may pop out
questions.answer: The water expands more than the glass, pressure builds up, and the bottle may crack or the stopper may pop out

questions.question: An experiment uses a metal rod with one end fixed. As the rod is heated, the free end pushes against a dial gauge. What is being measured?
options: The linear expansion of the rod
The colour change of the rod
questions.answer: The linear expansion of the rod

questions.question: In an experiment to compare the expansion of different metals, why must all rods be the same length and heated by the same temperature increase?
options: To make it a fair test - controlling variables so only the type of metal affects the expansion
questions.answer: To make it a fair test - controlling variables so only the type of metal affects the expansion

questions.question: A flask fitted with a narrow tube is filled with coloured water. When the flask is placed in hot water, the water level in the tube first drops slightly, then rises. Explain this observation.
options: First, the glass flask expands (increasing volume, so liquid level drops), then the liquid expands (more than glass), causing the level to rise
questions.answer: First, the glass flask expands (increasing volume, so liquid level drops), then the liquid expands (more than glass), causing the level to rise

questions.question: A round-bottomed flask has a narrow glass tube attached. The flask is inverted with the open end of the tube under water. When hands are placed around the flask, bubbles emerge from the tube. Explain why.
options: The air inside the flask is heated by the hands, expands, and is pushed out as bubbles
questions.answer: The air inside the flask is heated by the hands, expands, and is pushed out as bubbles

questions.question: A student states: 'When a metal is heated, the particles get bigger.' Is this correct?
options: No. The particles themselves do not change size. They gain kinetic energy, vibrate more, and move further apart, increasing the overall volume
questions.answer: No. The particles themselves do not change size. They gain kinetic energy, vibrate more, and move further apart, increasing the overall volume

questions.question: A student states: 'When a metal plate with a hole is heated, the hole gets smaller because the metal expands into the hole.' Is this correct?
options: No. The hole also expands. The metal expands in all directions, including the material around the hole, making the hole larger
No, but only because the metal melts
questions.answer: No. The hole also expands. The metal expands in all directions, including the material around the hole, making the hole larger

questions.question: A student states: 'A bimetallic strip bends because one metal gets heavier when heated.' Is this correct?
options: No. Mass does not change with temperature. The strip bends because one metal expands more than the other, causing the strip to curve
No, but it bends because one metal melts
questions.answer: No. Mass does not change with temperature. The strip bends because one metal expands more than the other, causing the strip to curve

questions.question:
A glass thermometer has a bulb containing mercury connected to a narrow capillary tube. Explain how and why each of these design features helps the thermometer work effectively:

(a) Thin glass bulb
(b) Mercury liquid
(c) Narrow capillary tube
options: (a) For strength; (b) Because it's cheap; (c) To look nice
(a) Allows heat to conduct quickly to mercury; (b) Mercury expands uniformly with temperature; (c) Small volume change gives large height change for sensitivity
(a) To break easily; (b) Because it's silver; (c) To use less glass
All features are for appearance only
questions.answer: (a) Allows heat to conduct quickly to mercury; (b) Mercury expands uniformly with temperature; (c) Small volume change gives large height change for sensitivity

questions.question: On a cold winter day, you notice that a steel bridge appears to have gaps at the expansion joints, but on a hot summer day, the gaps are much smaller. Explain this observation.
options: In cold weather, the steel contracts, widening the gaps. In hot weather, the steel expands, narrowing the gaps. The joints allow this movement without damaging the bridge
People remove parts of the bridge in winter
The gaps are an optical illusion
questions.answer: In cold weather, the steel contracts, widening the gaps. In hot weather, the steel expands, narrowing the gaps. The joints allow this movement without damaging the bridge

questions.question: A car tyre pressure gauge shows higher pressure on a hot day after driving compared to a cold morning before driving. Explain using your knowledge of gas expansion.
options: More air has entered the tyre
Driving heats the tyres and the air inside. The heated air expands and exerts greater pressure on the tyre walls
questions.answer: Driving heats the tyres and the air inside. The heated air expands and exerts greater pressure on the tyre walls

questions.question: A bimetallic strip in a thermostat is made of copper (high expansion) bonded to invar (low expansion). At a set temperature, the strip is straight and touching a contact to complete a circuit. When the temperature rises above the set point, what happens?
options: The strip bends towards the copper side and maintains contact
The strip bends towards the invar side (copper expands more, so copper is on outside of curve), breaking contact and switching off the heater
questions.answer: The strip bends towards the invar side (copper expands more, so copper is on outside of curve), breaking contact and switching off the heater

questions.question:
A metal rod is 2.000 m long at 20°C. When heated to 100°C, it expands to 2.002 m. 

(a) What is the expansion of the rod?
(b) If a second identical rod is heated from 20°C to 60°C, what would be its approximate length? (Assume uniform expansion)
options: (a) 0.002 m; (b) 2.001 m (half the temperature rise = half the expansion)
questions.answer: (a) 0.002 m; (b) 2.001 m (half the temperature rise = half the expansion)
